1:
The chapter opens with a king turning wisdom into work, and the witnesses read Solomon’s double resolve — a house for the LORD and a house for his kingdom — as the measure of his priorities. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry seizes on the order of the two projects. Solomon’s wisdom, he says, was given not merely for speculation, to entertain himself or his friends, but for action; and to action he at once applies himself, determining to build in the first place a house for the name of the LORD. It is fit that He who is first should be served first — a temple, and then a palace — and even the royal house Solomon designed not so much for his own convenience or magnitude as for the kingdom, for its honour among its neighbours and the decent reception of the people who came to their prince. In both, Henry insists, he aimed at the public good: “We are not born for ourselves, but for God and our country,” and the wisest men are those who lay out themselves most for the honour of God’s name and the welfare of communities.

The reformed John Gill keeps to the first house, glossing Solomon’s determination to build for the name of the LORD as a resolve for the worship and service of God, for his honour and glory, to which he had been directed, enjoined, and encouraged by his father David. The two wesleyan-arminian voices divide the verse’s two buildings between them: Adam Clarke reads “a house for the name of the LORD” as a temple for the worship of Jehovah and “a house for his kingdom” as a royal palace for Solomon’s own use as king of Israel, while John Wesley takes the latter as a palace for himself and his successors.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the single Hebrew word behind “determined.” Literally it is “said,” he notes, which may mean either commanded — as at 2 Chron. 1:2 and 1 Chron. 21:17 — or thought, purposed, resolved, as in the parallel at 1 Kings 5:5; and the context, he judges, favours the latter sense. “An house for his kingdom” he renders “for his royalty,” that is, a palace for himself, mentioned again at 2 Chron. 7:11 and 8:1, though the Chronicler, unlike the older record at 1 Kings 7:1-12, never relates its building. The witnesses do not divide; together they set Solomon before us as a king whose first act of wisdom is worship, and whose second is the ordering of a realm for the good of its people.
2:
With the bare tally of the levy — seventy thousand to bear burdens, eighty thousand to hew in the mountains, three thousand six hundred to oversee them — the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch pause to survey the whole design the chapter is unfolding. Here, they observe, begins the account of the preparations Solomon made for his buildings, and above all for the temple, opening with the notice that the king caused all the workmen of the necessary sort in his kingdom to be numbered. There follows the negotiation with Hiram of Tyre over the sending of a skilful architect and the materials — cedar wood and hewn stones from Lebanon — and then, in conclusion, the statements about the levying of the statute-labourers are taken up again and rendered more complete. Comparing the parallel at 1 Kings 5:5 and following, they note that there the negotiation is preceded by a notice that Hiram, hearing of Solomon’s accession, had sent an embassy to congratulate him; the Chronicler omits it, they explain, because it had no importance for the negotiations that succeeded.

The reformed John Gill fastens instead on a number, and on a difficulty he is content to defer. “Solomon told out threescore and ten thousand men,” he writes, and points the reader on to the parallel place for the account of these figures and for the difference of the last of them — the tally of the overseers — from the number given in the older narrative, a discrepancy he does not resolve at this verse but leaves to be settled where the fuller notice stands. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, for his part, treats the verse chiefly as a hinge, marking that with it the historian turns to the treaty with Huram of Tyre, the transaction that will occupy the body of the chapter.

So the witnesses, brief as they are here, agree in reading the muster not as an end in itself but as the first stroke of a larger enterprise. The numbering of this vast body of workmen is the practical beginning of the temple, the point at which Solomon’s resolve of the opening verse begins to take on hands and tools; and the very figures that will trouble the harmonist at the chapter’s close are set down here without comment, as the plain record of how great a labour the house of God required. The reader is meant to feel the scale of the undertaking, and to be carried forward at once into the embassy that will secure its cedar, its craftsman, and its stone.
3:
“And Solomon sent to Huram king of Tyre” — the witnesses gather around the name, the back-story, and the appeal to an old friendship. The reformed John Gill notes first that this Huram is the same as Hiram, and that a letter had already passed the other way: Huram had first written to congratulate Solomon on his accession to the throne, a courtesy the Chronicler does not here record. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke turns to the spelling, observing that the king’s name is written Chiram in the book of Kings but Churam in Chronicles — no real difference, he says, only a yod and a vau interchanged, as he had explained at 1 Kings 5:2.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gathers the historical and textual detail. He too directs the reader to the parallel at 1 Kings 5:2-11, from which we learn that Huram had first sent to congratulate Solomon; the account here agrees generally with that older passage, and the variations, he judges, only prove that the Chronicler made independent use of his sources. In Kings the name is spelt Hiram and Hirom; and Ellicott finds it interesting that a king of Tyre bore this name again in the days of Tiglath-pileser II, paying tribute — as the Assyrian records show, under the form Hi-ru-um-mu — alongside Menahem of Samaria. On the clause “as thou didst deal with David,” he notes that the translators rightly supplied the words “even so deal with me” to complete the sense, following the Vulgate’s “sic fac mecum”; and he adds that the parallel at 1 Kings 5:3 makes Solomon refer to the wars which had hindered David from building the temple.

The witnesses do not contend. Between them they set the embassy in its proper light: a young king writing to his father’s old ally, trading on a kindness already shown to David and lately renewed in Huram’s own message of congratulation. Gill and Ellicott alike stress that the friendship was mutual and prior — Huram had made the first approach — so that Solomon’s letter is not a cold demand but the taking up of a bond that reached back a generation. And Clarke’s small philological care, that the differing spellings hide one and the same man, guards the reader against imagining two kings where there was only one, the same Hiram who had loved David now ready to serve David’s son.
4:
Solomon’s letter now states the temple’s purpose — “Behold, I build an house to the name of the LORD my God, to dedicate it to him” — and the witnesses trace the catalogue of worship the house was to shelter. The reformed John Gill reads the opening as a settled intention: Solomon is about to do it and is determined upon it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch take the participle in the same way — “behold, I will build,” the form used of what one is on the point of doing — and dwell on the infinitives that follow, which define the design of the whole. The temple was to be consecrated to God by worshipping him there in the manner he had prescribed: by burning the incense of sweet spices that Exodus 30:7 appoints for the morning and evening, by the continual pile of the shewbread of Exodus 25:30, and by the burnt offerings of morning and evening, of sabbaths, new moons, and the solemn feasts, all ordained in Numbers 28 and 29. “This is an ordinance for ever to Israel.”

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott works over the same verse phrase by phrase. “I build” he renders “am about to build”; “to dedicate” he glosses as “to consecrate,” comparing Leviticus 27:14 and 1 Kings 9:3, and he would drop the italicised “and,” since the words that follow define the very purpose of the dedication — namely the burning of incense before him. The continual shewbread and the burnt offerings, he notes, are loosely bound in the Hebrew to the verb “to burn,” so that the sense runs: for offering before him incense of spices, and a continual pile of bread, and burnt offerings. “Solemn feasts” he takes as “set seasons,” the special sacrifices of which are prescribed from Numbers 28:9 onward; and the closing formula he reads literally, “for ever this is obligatory upon Israel,” the same standing phrase as the “statute for ever” so common in the law, as at Exodus 12:14.

The witnesses do not divide; the two grammatical-historical readers simply confirm and enrich each other, and Gill’s brief note of Solomon’s fixed resolve stands over both. Together they show that the house was not conceived as a monument but as a place of appointed service — every stone of it ordered toward the incense, the bread, and the offerings by which Israel was to keep covenant with her God. The greatness of the building, which the next verse will assert, is thus grounded first in the greatness of the worship it exists to house.
5:
“And the house which I build is great: for great is our God above all gods” — the witnesses weigh in what sense a house of stone can be called great, and ground its greatness not in its size but in its God. The reformed John Gill is careful with the word: the temple was not so very large, though with all its apartments and courts it was considerable; it is called great rather because it was magnificent in its structure and its decorations. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley makes the same distinction from the other side — the temple strictly so called was but small, yet the buildings belonging to it were large and numerous.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch turn the greatness outward and upward. The house must be great, they reason, precisely because Jahve is greater than all gods, as Exodus 18:11 and Deuteronomy 10:17 declare; and yet no one is able to build a house in which such a God could dwell, for the heaven of heavens cannot contain him — words that echo Solomon’s own prayer at 1 Kings 8:27 and 2 Chron. 6:18. The building, then, is not meant for God’s habitation but only for the offering of worship before him. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott agrees that the greatness is relative to God rather than to human scale, and injects a note of sober proportion: by modern notions of magnitude the temple of Solomon was a small building, and the poet’s fancy of “proud Salem’s haughty fane” rearing to heaven its thousand golden domes is pure imagination.

So the witnesses hold two truths in a single hand, and none of them finally divides from the rest. The house is great, and it is small: great in the veneration it expresses and the magnificence lavished on it, small beside the infinite God to whom it is raised and whom no temple could ever contain. Gill and Wesley locate the greatness in the splendour and the range of the sacred buildings; Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott set that greatness against the immeasurable majesty of the God worshipped there, so that the very grandeur of the work becomes a confession of its own insufficiency. The greatness of the house is the greatness of Israel’s esteem for a God whom the heavens themselves cannot hold — a thought the following verse will press home in Solomon’s own astonished question.
6:
“But who is able to build him an house, seeing the heaven and heaven of heavens cannot contain him?” — the witnesses hear in Solomon’s question not false modesty but a true theology of the God who cannot be housed. The reformed John Gill puts it plainly: who is able to build a house suitable to the greatness of God’s majesty, especially since he dwells not in temples made with hands? The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley catches the careful qualification Solomon makes of his own enterprise: when he speaks of building a house for our great God, let none think he means to comprehend God within it, for he is infinite; the house is raised only to sacrifice, that is, to worship him there where he is graciously present.

The other wesleyan-arminian voice, Adam Clarke, reaches for the Targum to expand the thought, which paraphrases: “For the lower heavens, the middle heavens, and the upper heavens cannot contain him, seeing he sustains all things by the arm of his power. Heaven is the throne of his glory, the earth his footstool... Who am I, then, that I should build him a house?” Clarke draws the same conclusion as Wesley: it is not under any hope that the house should be able to contain God, but merely for the purpose of burning incense and offering sacrifice, that Solomon undertakes to erect it. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the opening “who could keep strength?” and finds the high thought a reminiscence of Solomon’s prayer at 1 Kings 8:27 and 2 Chron. 6:18: Solomon is not so ignorant of the infinite nature of Deity as to think of localising it within an earthly dwelling. He builds not for God’s residence but for his worship and service; “to burn sacrifice” is literally “to burn incense,” used here in a general sense for the whole of worship.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, reading these verses as one, take the phrase “to burn incense” in the same way, as standing by a part for the whole service of the LORD, and pass at once from Solomon’s disclaimer to the request it introduces — the skilful workman and the costly timber that Hiram is now to supply. The witnesses do not divide. Every voice bends the same way: the temple is no cage for the Almighty but an appointed place of approach, and Solomon’s “Who am I?” is the fitting posture of a mighty king who knows that the God he serves is greater than the heavens, and cannot be shut within walls however magnificent.
7:
Solomon’s first specific request is for a master craftsman, and the witnesses attend both to the range of the man’s skill and to the workmen already waiting for him. The reformed John Gill explains the breadth of the commission — a man cunning to work in gold, silver, brass, and iron — by the many things relating to the temple, and the vessels to be set within it, that had to be made of those metals. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sharpens the point: Solomon asks not merely for a labourer but for a person of great ingenuity, one capable of planning and directing, who might be set over the other artists.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott works through the whole list. “Send me now” he reads as “and now send me a wise man, to work in the gold and in the silver,” recalling David’s provision at 1 Chron. 22:15. He lingers over the dyer’s and weaver’s arts named here — purple, crimson, and blue — noting that no allusion is made to this kind of work in the later description of the craftsman’s productions at 2 Chron. 4:11-16, nor in the parallel at 1 Kings 7:13 and following, which describe only his works in metal; the man’s versatile genius, he remarks, might easily be paralleled by famous names of the Renaissance. “Purple” he marks as an Aramaic form, “crimson” as a word of Persian origin found only here and at 2 Chron. 3:14 — the ancestor of our own “carmine” — and “blue” as the dark blue or violet of the sanctuary. “To grave” is literally “to carve carvings,” whether in wood or in stone, as at 1 Kings 6:29; and the closing clause, “with the cunning men that are with me in Judah and in Jerusalem,” the Hebrew binds back to the verb “to work,” so that the Tyrian master is to labour together with the artists whom David his father had already provided, as at 1 Chron. 29:2.

The witnesses do not divide. Gill and Clarke supply the reason and the rank of the request — skilled metalwork for a house full of sacred vessels, and a director set over the rest — while Ellicott unfolds the astonishing versatility Solomon looked for, a single artificer at home in metal and dye, wood and gem. And all three note, with the text, that Israel was not left without craftsmen of her own: David had gathered cunning men in Jerusalem, and the man of Tyre was sought not to replace them but to lead them, that temple-work might be the very best of its kind.
8:
“Send me also cedar trees, fir trees, and algum trees, out of Lebanon” — the request for timber draws the witnesses into questions of botany and geography, and one genuine difficulty. The reformed John Gill identifies the third wood: the algum trees are the same as the almug, the letters transposed. They cannot be coral, as some Jewish writers thought, for coral grows in the sea while these were on Lebanon; nor Brazil-wood, as Kimchi supposed, a thing not then known. Trees of almug were indeed afterwards brought from Ophir in India and from Arabia, and the word itself, Gill observes, seems to be Arabic, carrying the article “al” prefixed.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the point Gill’s note skirts. “Fir trees,” he says, are now often rendered cypresses, but the word rather denotes a pine, common on Lebanon. “Algum” he takes as sandal wood, its Hebrew spelling nearer the native Indian name than the form used at 1 Kings 10:11. And here is the trouble: the Chronicler asks for sandal wood “out of Lebanon,” though he himself knew, as 2 Chron. 9:10 shows, that it came from Ophir at the mouth of the Indus. The desire to be concise, Ellicott suggests, has betrayed him into an inaccuracy of statement — unless Solomon himself, knowing sandal wood only as a Phoenician import, supposed it grew like the cedars on Lebanon; and the divergence between this account and the older one leaves it doubtful, he adds, whether either preserves more than an ideal sketch of Solomon’s message.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the syntax and the bargain. The infinitive that follows, they explain, is subordinate to the request: send me cedars, which thy people, skilful at the work, hew, and in which my servants shall assist them, so as to prepare building timber in plenty. And they note what the older narrative omits — that Solomon here promises, in return, to supply the Tyrian woodcutters with grain, wine, and oil for their maintenance, the provision spelled out in the verses that follow. So the witnesses divide only in their confidence about the record: Gill and Keil and Delitzsch take the request at face value, tracing the woods and the labour-sharing, while Ellicott honestly flags the puzzle of sandal wood sought from a mountain that never grew it — whether the slip is the Chronicler’s or Solomon’s own, a small crack through which the historian’s method shows.
9:
“Even to prepare me timber in abundance: for the house which I am about to build shall be wonderful great” — the witnesses read the verse as the summing-up of the timber request and a fresh confession of the temple’s grandeur. The reformed John Gill explains why so vast a quantity of wood was needed: Solomon would require it for the raftering, the ceiling, the wainscoting, and the flooring of the house, a building that would swallow timber on a great scale.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the grammar. “Even to prepare me timber” he would render rather “and they shall prepare,” or “let them prepare” — a use of the infinitive to which, he notes, the Chronicler is markedly partial, and which he traces through a string of other places in these books; the Syriac catches the same sense with “let them be bringing to me.” For “wonderful great” he cites the Septuagint’s “great and glorious” and the Syriac’s “an astonishment,” so that the phrase reaches after a magnificence words can barely hold. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, whose comment runs on through the following verses, read the same clause as the ground of the whole request — timber in abundance, because the house was to be wonderful great — and then pass to the wages Solomon pledges and to Hiram’s reply, which they treat together.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse’s force lies in its repetition. Twice now Solomon has called his house great — once for the greatness of his God, and now for the abundance of material its greatness demands — and the two are finally the same claim in different keys. Gill grounds the abundance in the sheer physical reach of the building, floor and wall and roof all to be clothed in cedar; Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch hear in “wonderful great” the same note of astonished magnificence that has run through the letter from its opening. The timber is ordered without stint because the work is beyond ordinary measure, and the reader is carried on at once to the wages by which Solomon means to keep so great an army of woodcutters at their task.
10:
“Behold, I will give to thy servants, the hewers that cut timber, twenty thousand measures of beaten wheat” — Solomon names the wages, and the witnesses probe both the grain and the recipients. The reformed John Gill weighs the phrase “beaten wheat.” It means neither wheat beaten out of the husk with a flail, nor grain bruised for pottage, but rather flour ground fine, as Rabbi Jonah took it, or better still simply “food” for the household — and Gill notes why the wages were paid in this kind: wheat was scarce among the Tyrians, who were obliged to have it from the Jews.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott recasts the whole verse and, in doing so, raises a question about who was fed. He would render it: “as for the hewers, the woodcutters, I will give wheat as food for thy servants, twenty thousand kors, and barley twenty thousand kors.” So read, the verse falls into harmony with the parallel at 1 Kings 5:11, where Solomon gives Hiram twenty thousand kors of wheat “as food for his household,” year by year through the building. The very words “beaten wheat,” Ellicott argues, rest on a misreading: the Hebrew is literally “wheat—strokes,” but the second word is a slip for the term meaning “food,” exactly the word used in Kings and rendered so by the Septuagint. On this reckoning “thy servants” here answers to “his household” there, and the drift of the passage is that, in return for the Tyrian artificers, Solomon undertakes to supply Hiram’s own court with corn and wine and oil. Others, Ellicott reports, suppose instead two distinct agreements — one to provision the king’s household, another the labourers — but he judges that without much likelihood.

So a real question stands beneath the verse: were these great stores of grain the hire of the woodcutters in the forest, or the yearly tribute of provisions to Hiram’s palace? The plain surface of the text, which Gill largely follows, points them to the hewers themselves; Ellicott’s harmonising with Kings turns them toward the royal household, and dismisses the compromise of two separate bargains. On the measures the witnesses agree with the text and with each other: the kor was a dry measure and the bath a liquid one, the same words, Ellicott observes, that meet us again in the Greek of the parable at Luke 16:6-7. Whether court or labourers were fed, the scale is princely — twenty thousand measures of each — and it shows a king who meant his workmen, and his ally, to want for nothing while the house of God rose.
11:
“Then Huram the king of Tyre answered in writing, which he sent to Solomon” — the witnesses read the Tyrian king’s reply as a model of neighbourly courtesy and, in one voice, as a small window onto the ancient world. The reformed John Gill notes simply that in his letter Huram told Solomon he would grant all that he desired. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry dwells on the grace of the reply, in which Huram shows great respect for Solomon and a readiness to serve him — meaner people, he adds, may learn from these great ones to be neighbourly and obliging. Above all Henry fastens on the congratulation, “Because the LORD loved his people, he hath made thee king over them.” A wise and good government, he observes, is a great blessing to a people and may well be reckoned a singular token of God’s favour; and it is worth marking that Huram does not say “because he loved thee he made thee king,” though that was true, but “because he loved his people.” Princes must look on themselves as preferred for the public good and not for their own satisfaction, and should so rule as to prove that they were given in love and not in anger.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke seizes on the two words “in writing” for a longer reflection. Even in these early times, he notes, kings could write and read among nations the Greeks and Romans were pleased to call barbarous; and he sets against this the irony that nearly two thousand years later a king sat on the British throne who could not sign his own name, and that about the year 700 Withred king of Kent confirmed a charter with the sign of the cross “because I cannot write.” The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the same phrase more narrowly: “answered in writing” is “said in a letter,” which seems to imply that Solomon’s own message had been delivered orally. He compares the parallel, where, on hearing Solomon’s request, Huram first blesses Jehovah before setting down his reply, as at 1 Kings 10:9.

The witnesses do not divide. Gill and Henry take the letter as the warm assent of a friendly king, Henry drawing from it a lesson for all who rule — that a throne is a trust held for the people’s good; Clarke turns the mere fact of a written answer into a meditation on the dignity of these supposedly barbarous courts; and Ellicott fixes the small historical detail that Solomon’s embassy was spoken and Huram’s reply was penned. Together they show a heathen king honouring the God of Israel and the king God had given, and doing it, remarkably, with pen and letter across the sea.
12:
“Blessed be the LORD God of Israel, that made heaven and earth” — Huram’s doxology sets the witnesses to asking how much true knowledge of God lay behind a Tyrian king’s words. The reformed John Gill takes them at face value: Huram seems to have had some good notions of the divine Being, not merely as the God of Israel in a peculiar sense but as the Former and Maker of all things. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley goes further, judging that Huram was not only a friend to the Jewish nation but a proselyte to their religion, worshipping Jehovah — the God now known by that name to the neighbouring nations — as the God that made heaven and earth and the very fountain of power and of being.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott hears the same confession with a more critical ear. In the older account at 1 Kings 5:7, he points out, Huram says only, “Blessed be the LORD this day, which hath given unto David a wise son over this great people”; the fuller ascription to the Maker of heaven and earth is peculiar to Chronicles, and the Chronicler, Ellicott suggests, may have shaped his source in a monotheistic direction. Yet he allows the other possibility — that Jehovah was genuinely known to the Phoenician under the title “Maker of heaven and earth,” a form of words he compares with the blessing of Genesis 14:19 and with a royal inscription of Xerxes that hails “the great god Ahuramazda... who made this earth, who made these heavens.” The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, reading Huram’s letter as a whole, simply follow its course: the king blesses the LORD for giving David so wise a son to build a house for the LORD and a house for his kingdom, and passes on to announce the craftsman he is sending.

So the witnesses genuinely divide over the heart behind the words. Gill and Wesley take Huram’s blessing as the confession of a real, if partial, faith — a heathen king turned worshipper of Israel’s God; Ellicott holds the question open, weighing whether the lofty monotheism belongs to Huram himself or to the historian who reports him. None of them doubts the truth the words assert, that the God of Israel made heaven and earth; they differ only on whose theology we are hearing — the Tyrian’s, or the Chronicler’s shaping of the Tyrian’s — a difference that leaves the verse both a genuine tribute from outside Israel and a token of the writer’s own confessing hand.
13:
“And now I have sent a cunning man, endued with understanding, of Huram my father’s” — the short notice introducing the master craftsman turns, in the witnesses’ hands, on the meaning of a single word. The reformed John Gill reads the description straightforwardly: Huram has sent a cunning man, endued with understanding in just such things as Solomon had required of him. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke supplies the man’s name, gathering it from the parallel: he was called Hiram, or Hiram Abi, as the notes on 1 Kings 7:13-14 show.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott parts company with Clarke precisely here. “Endued with understanding” he matches with the same phrase at 1 Chron. 12:32; but the words “of Huram my father’s” he would render rather “Huram my father” — taking “father” not as part of a proper name but as a title of honour, meaning master or preceptor, exactly as the same craftsman is called the “father” of Solomon at 2 Chron. 4:16. He compares the like use of “father” as an honorific title of rank or reverence at Genesis 45:8, Judges 17:10, and Judges 18:19, and notes that the Septuagint and Vulgate read it so, while the Syriac omits it altogether. On his reading the word is no surname at all but a mark of the craftsman’s eminence, the same deference Solomon himself would later be shown to owe the man.

So a real, if quiet, disagreement runs under the verse: is “Huram-abi” the artist’s actual name, Hiram Abi, as Clarke has it from the book of Kings, or is “abi” — “my father” — a courtesy title fixed to a craftsman named simply Huram, as Ellicott argues from the parallel usage in Chronicles? Clarke hears a name; Ellicott hears an honour paid to a master workman. Gill, content to leave the man’s designation aside, dwells only on his fitness — that he was endued with understanding for the very tasks Solomon had named, and so answered exactly to the request of the earlier verses. The witnesses agree entirely on the substance: the man Tyre sent was skilled and understanding, dispatched expressly to meet Solomon’s requests and to lead the Israelite artisans already gathered. They differ only on whether the word appended to his name marks his kindred or his standing — whether we should call him Hiram Abi, the man’s own name, or Huram the master, the title of an honoured chief of the works.
14:
The letter now describes the craftsman’s parentage and his powers — “the son of a woman of the daughters of Dan, and his father was a man of Tyre” — and the witnesses meet at once the old discrepancy with the parallel record. The reformed John Gill flags it and defers, noting that here we have the account of the artificer’s descent and skill, and that the seeming disagreement between this verse and the fuller notice may be resolved as he had shown at 1 Kings 7:14.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lays the difficulty out. In 1 Kings 7:14 the man is called the son of a widow of the tribe of Naphtali, but here of the daughters of Dan. He reports Bertheau’s harmonising: she was by birth a Danite, married into the tribe of Naphtali, was there widowed, and as a widow of Naphtali became the wife of a man of Tyre, by whom she bore this son — so that two of the tribes of Israel could each claim him on his mother’s side. Yet Ellicott offers a simpler possibility, that in the Hebrew “daughters of Dan” we may be seeing a corruption of the word Naphtali. On the man’s gifts he is careful: the epithet “skilful” belongs to Huram himself, not to his Tyrian father; and where 1 Kings 7:14 calls him simply a worker in brass, the Chronicler adds skill in gold, silver, iron, stone, timber, purple, blue, fine linen, and crimson, and the graving of every device — Huram, he remarks, being naturally represented here as enhancing the accomplishments of his artist. The phrase “to find out every device which shall be put to him” he renders “to devise any manner of device that may be given him,” a reminiscence of the endowment of Bezalel at Exodus 31:4; and the closing “my lord David” he marks as a touch of Oriental politeness, since Huram stood independent of David as of Solomon.

So the witnesses divide chiefly over the tribe. Was the mother a Danite or a Naphtalite? Ellicott offers both a harmony — a Danite woman twice married, first into Naphtali and then to a Tyrian — and the blunter suggestion of a textual slip; Gill simply points to his own reconciliation at the parallel. Neither doubts the man himself: half Israelite and half Gentile, a master of metal and dye, wood and stone, sent from Tyre to labour on the house of Israel’s God. The mingled parentage that puzzles the harmonist is, on every reading, both of Israel and of the nations — the one point on which Gill and Ellicott, for all their difference over the tribe, entirely agree.
15:
“Now therefore the wheat, and the barley, the oil, and the wine, which my lord hath spoken of, let him send unto his servants” — with these words Huram closes the bargain, and the witnesses, though only two speak here, catch the courtesy folded into the terms. The reformed John Gill lingers over the title “my lord.” Some, he notes, think Huram speaks so because he was tributary to Solomon and stood in a subject’s relation to him; but it may just as well be, Gill judges, only a respectful way of speaking, the deference of one king writing graciously to another rather than any confession of vassalage.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the verse as Huram’s plain acceptance of the exchange Solomon had proposed — the grain and wine and oil named back in the earlier offer are simply to be sent, and the Tyrian king takes the terms as they stand. The word “his servants,” Ellicott observes, Huram means of himself and his court; it is the exact correlative of the “lord” he has just used, so that the two titles frame the transaction in the polite idiom of royal correspondence — Solomon the “lord” who provides, Huram and his people the “servants” who receive.

The witnesses do not divide. Between them they show a negotiation brought smoothly to its close: Solomon had offered provisions for the woodcutters, and Huram now asks only that they be sent, accepting the arrangement without haggling. Gill’s care over “my lord” guards the reader from reading too much into a courtesy — the word need not mean that Tyre was subject to Israel — while Ellicott’s note on “his servants” shows the same courtesy answered in kind from the other side. What might have been a hard commercial contract reads, in the event, as an exchange of civilities between friendly crowns: the one supplying corn and wine, the other cedar and skill, each addressing the other in the gracious language of lord and servant. The stage is thus set for the timber to come down from Lebanon, and the last practical question — how it should travel — is what the next verse takes up.
16:
“And we will cut wood out of Lebanon, as much as thou shalt need” — the closing promise of Huram’s letter concerns the shipping of the timber, and the witnesses trace the route from the mountain to the holy city. The reformed John Gill notes briefly that the wood in view is both cedar and fir, the timber Solomon had asked for. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, on the phrase “in floats by sea to Joppa,” points the reader to his fuller notes at 1 Kings 5:9 and the parallel places for the details of the arrangement.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott works over nearly every word. The “we” is emphatic in the Hebrew — “we, on our part, will cut” — the pronoun deliberately expressed. “As much as thou shalt need” turns on a word for “need” that occurs only here in the Old Testament, though it is common in the Targums and later writing, where the older narrative at 1 Kings 5:8 had used the classical phrase “all thy desire.” “In floats” renders another isolated term, which the Greek and Latin versions take as “rafts” — a meaning settled, Ellicott says, by the plainer word for rafts at 1 Kings 5:9. “To Joppa” makes definite what the older account had left vague as “the place that thou shalt appoint me”; Joppa was the harbour nearest Jerusalem. And “thou shalt carry it up to Jerusalem” spells out the curt phrase of Kings, “thou shalt take them away.”

From this close comparison Ellicott draws two conclusions the other witnesses do not dispute: first, that the Chronicler plainly wrote with knowledge of the older text, interpreting and expanding its terse clauses; and second, that neither text preserves the exact form of the original documents, but each gives its own working of the record. He notes, with Josephus, that some memorial of these negotiations between Huram and Solomon was said to be still extant at Tyre in the historian’s own day. The witnesses do not divide. Gill and Clarke are content to identify the timber and point to the parallel; Ellicott shows how the two accounts stand to one another, the later filling out the earlier — and both agree on the plain sense, that Tyre would fell the trees, float them down the coast to Joppa, and leave to Solomon the last stage of carrying them up into the hills to Jerusalem.
17:
“And Solomon numbered all the strangers that were in the land of Israel” — the narrative returns from the letters to the labour force, and the witnesses ask who these “strangers” were and why they were counted. The reformed John Gill identifies them, following Kimchi, as the remnant of the old Canaanite peoples — Amorites, Hittites, Perizzites, Jebusites — but not as idolaters, for such would not have been suffered to dwell in the land; rather they were proselytes of the gate, resident aliens who had come under the God of Israel. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley explains the double numbering: David had numbered not only his own people but afterward the strangers, that Solomon might have a true account of them and employ them in his buildings; yet Solomon numbered them again, because death might have made a considerable alteration among them since David’s reckoning.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott agrees that “the strangers” are the indigenous Canaanite population, the sense the word carries as far back as Genesis 23:4 and Leviticus 17:8. He notes that the term for the “numbering” or census occurs only here in the Old Testament, and that the earlier census “wherewith David his father had numbered them” is briefly recorded at 1 Chron. 22:2 — the subject population having been made liable to forced labour already under David, as the notice of Adoram “over the levy” at 2 Samuel 20:24 shows. And he adds up the figures: the total found, one hundred and fifty-three thousand six hundred, is exactly the sum of the numbers assigned in the following verse.

The witnesses do not divide. Gill and Ellicott agree on who the strangers were — the old inhabitants of Canaan, now resident aliens rather than idolatrous enemies — and Wesley supplies the reason for a fresh count, that the rolls David had taken needed correcting for the deaths of the intervening years. Together they show Solomon doing exactly what a great building demanded: taking a careful census of the available hands before setting them to their tasks. The bare arithmetic carries its own lesson, which Gill will draw out at the next verse — that this vast body of the nations, gathered within Israel, was now bent to the service of the house of Israel’s God.
18:
The chapter closes with the muster set to its tasks — “threescore and ten thousand to bear burdens, and fourscore thousand to hew in the mountain, and three thousand and six hundred overseers” — and the witnesses attend to the numbers, the omissions, and the meaning. The reformed John Gill takes the verse as a repetition of the count given earlier, showing how the strangers were disposed: seventy thousand bearers, eighty thousand hewers, three thousand six hundred overseers, one hundred and fifty-three thousand six hundred in all. And he cannot leave the figures without a spiritual reading: this vast levy of the nations, employed on the temple, he takes as an emblem of the Gentiles employed in building the spiritual temple, the church.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott checks the numbers against the parallel and finds one difference to weigh. The seventy thousand and eighty thousand exactly agree with 1 Kings 5:15; but where this verse gives three thousand six hundred overseers, the older record at 1 Kings 5:16 reads three thousand three hundred. In the Hebrew, Ellicott observes, the words for “three” and “six” are easily confused, and he judges the Chronicler’s reading here to be the right one. He notes too that the Chronicler has passed over the levy of thirty thousand native Israelites which the parallel records — whether by oversight or from some disapproval, he cannot say — and remarks that, were that number added, the whole body would reach a hundred and eighty thousand, a company of fifty men for each of the thirty-six hundred overseers. The words “to set the people a work,” he adds, mean simply to make the people work.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fill in around this. Keil and Delitzsch give the plain sense of the distribution, the bearers and hewers and overseers set each to their charge; Wesley, glossing “hewers in the mountains,” explains the policy behind it — that Solomon would not employ the free-born Israelites in this drudgery, but the strangers who were proselytes, men who, having no lands of their own, applied themselves to trades and got their living by their industry. So the witnesses close the chapter without real division. They agree on the disposition of the workmen and on the reason the burden fell on the resident aliens; they differ only over a single digit — three hundred against six hundred overseers — which Ellicott settles in the Chronicler’s favour, and over whether the omission of the Israelite levy was accident or design. And over the whole stands Gill’s larger thought: that this gathering of the nations to raise the house of God is a shadow of the church to come, built at last of Jew and Gentile together.
